author: @BanjoLuke1
text:
I'll never know, but I wonder sometimes whether Rains' character was so named because of Louis Renault and his company.
I'm not sure how widely Renault's passive (?) collaboration with the German occupiers was known in Hollywood at the time this film was written/made.
However... Captain Renault lives in Vichy Casablanca in the very same fog of deceit and pretence that Louis Renault used to profit from occupation. 
I may be wrong.
But I do wonder...

author: @rizqbeckett7086
text: What’s amazing about this movie is the things that I notice almost every time I watch it. I noticed that Ilsa has never told her husband that she loves him, although he often declares his love to her and not only in words, but she does say it to Rick. But there’s no denying that she admires her husband and it’s apparent why, in spite of the threats from the Nazis his courage and commitment is outstanding. There’s an incident that causes Rick to switch his mind from his original plan to allow Victor to get away alone without his wife. I think it’s when Victor unselfishly asks Rick to use the letters of transits to take his wife away after telling Rick to his face that he knows that he loves his wife too. I also think that Rick hated when Louie compared his low immoral self to him. The key word was when he asked her to trust him. Because in the next scene when Louie tried to arrest Victor….well the rest of the movie is perhaps one of the greatest endings in movie history with four words before walking away with Louie, “Here’s looking at you kid!”.

author: @jackrice2770
text:
I see a few film students have been assigned this film. They should note a couple of important things: 

1) Note there isn't any 'filler', not one unnecessary scene. 

2) Instead of the modern film style with naked, sweaty bodies flopping around for several minutes, here we see symbols and subtle shots that tell us that yes, Ilsa and Rick "do it" but we're spared the pace-killing soft-core porn so prevalent today. (That shot of the searchlight when the camera cuts away). Yes, Bogie and Ilsa are wearing clothes in the next shot, but apparently folks in the '40s were a bit quicker on the uptake than today. 

3) The script is tight, and although at least three writers worked on it, the final result works perfectly. The sign of a director in complete control of his project.

4) The shadows and camera angles are works of genius. Lighting designers and cinematographers should watch it frame by frame.

author: @jackrice2770
text:
Yes, in case you're a young'un, this movie has been voted one of the best by any number of film associations, One historical note: Renault says to Strasser that "he was with the Americans when they blundered into Berlin in 1918." Germany was never invaded during the First World War, and certainly no foreign troops, American or otherwise, entered the capital. Of course, one shouldn't rely on films for historical facts (actually, almost all 'historical' films are grossly inaccurate).

Here's looking at you, Kid.

author: @timothydavis1885
text: There have been few movies that have equaled the romantic twists, the suspense, the drama, the musical soundtrack, as Casablanca.   As Time Goes By will go down in cinematic history as one of the greatest musical masterpieces of all time.   This was undoubtedly Humphrey Bogart's greatest work. Humphrey Bogart was unquestionably one of the greatest actors ever to grace movie screen.  Ingrid Bergman will forever be one of the most beautiful woman ever to cross Hollywood, whose theatrical performance in Casablanca seemed as though she were living out her own life, which just happened to be portrayed in this timeless work of cinematic art.   Paul Heinreid was the ultimate patriot, a part he played so well, it would go down as one of the most realistic performances ever.  This movie is without doubt one of the top 10 movies Hollywood ever produced.

text: Rick wasn't helping Victor and Ilsa until she explained her train station no-show. He finally got that, then had to get Victor out of Captain Reynaud's custody and set up the pair's clean getaway. Rick realized that him and Ilsa having the hots for each other paled beside the importance of Victor's mission, and that staying together in Casablanca was impossibly dangerous. The script writers made sure Rick didn't have any easy outs, just a tough one.

author: @anglophils645
text: This one should never be remade.  No one could come close to duplicating the performances of Bogart, Bergman, Henried, or Rains.  These actors each had such a unique style------I don't believe that any combination of actors today could come together the way that they did.  The re-make of "Sebrina" fell flat.  Even with stars like Harrison Ford, Richard Kinnear, and Julia Ormond, the 1995 version does not compare to the 1954 original, starring Humphrey Bogart, William Holden, and Audrey Hepburn.  I fear the same would happen with an attempt to re-make "Casablanca".

author: @anglophils645
text: @tiffsaverThe only classic that I can think of, where the re-make is possibly as good or better than the original, is Pride and Prejudice.  The original starring Greer Garson and Lawrence Olivier, is not necessarily better than the 1995 version starring Colin Firth and Jennifer Ehle.  The 2005 version starring Keira Knightley and Donald Sutherland, among others, is also very good.

author: @tiffsaver
text:
@anglophils645 
Same thing with sequels.  But for my money, the best, and very first sequel of all time is "The Bride of Frankenstein," in 1935.

author: @Just_another_Euro_dude
text:
Old classic movies are still fantastic as any from the current age. Maybe few tiny details change throughout the decades, maybe a bit of mentality changes, and a LOT of technology changes, but the ESSENCE of the quality REMAINS, regardless if it's 1920s, 30s, 40s ,50s, 60s ,70s, 80s, 90s, 00s, 10s or 20s (again 😅)...
Few years ago i was naive enough to believe that old black and white movies are somehow inferior to the more modern ones, let's say starting from the 1980s and forward. But then i started watching a lot of the classics from the golden age of Hollywood and from the European cinema and from the Japanese cinema and boy i was so wrong. Watched the Bicycle thieves, watched the Seven Samurai, watched the White Heat with James Cagney, and Public Enemy, and also Scarface with Paul Muni. The method of the acting might be somewhat strange in those early 1930s movies i admit, (the infamous overacting) Public Enemy and The Scarface, but boy the classics from the 1940s and 1950s are just perfect. What Kurosawa did with The Seven samurai in 1954 was just an incredible achievement,  (almost a PERFECTION of a movie) and the emotion and warmth of the Italian Bicycle thieves from the 1948 is just something i didn't find in ANY movie of those times. It's amazing really. Classic is a classic regardless of the year but you need a bit of the life experience to realise that. It was impossible for me to realise that in my 20s or even in most of my 30s. I was the modern movies elitist. And was so soooo wrong. 😂 While interestingly enough i loved watching the old movies as a kid. Now in my early 40s i can't get enough of the old classics while STILL loving the modern cinema.

Fun fact...high quality black-and-white films from the 1930s had at least 10k resolution by modern digital standards.
Even after the original is scanned in 4K the result still would not be as "sharp" as on the actual movie screen 80+ years ago.
(Have a look at  your ancient family photos....if  the camera was focussed correctly every hair on your great- grandma's head was in  sharp focus.)

I totally understand the travel tiredness. Burnout happens to the best of us. Yes, you get to travel for a living, but at the same time, it's also work, and because it's work, it may drain your energy to actually enjoy what you wanted to do in the first place, travel. But thanks for being real with us! (And if you ever needed a break from posting videos, we'd understand! Mental health is important). Still, looking forward to your next adventure after this Morocco series. You def made Morocco high on my list of places to visit next. 

Rabat almost seems quiet and peaceful, and not so many people, which is always interesting for a capital city.

author: @Boug0014
text: It’s quite interesting with those street guides.  Some (I’m not saying all), don’t even know the history …. The Portuguese never occupied Rabat…. They occupied Maamoura in 1515 about 30km north of Rabat and Casablanca in also 1515.  But they never were in Rabat.  The Oudaya Kasbah was built by the Almohad Royal Family in 1150 as a fortification (Ribat) to fight the Berghwata Kingdom that extended from south of Rabat to about Essaouira.  The Kasbah was later used as a settlement for the Andalus Refugees after the fall of Granada and the expulsion of the Moriscos from Spain.

author: @J_Ges
text:
Hi Chris, compliments on your videos, I really enjoy them!
I’m a street photographer living in London and in March I’m going for a week in Marrakech. I’ve heard that sometimes people can be a bit hostile if they notice they’re being photographed, or may ask for money. I always try to be very respectful, quiet and discreet when I shoot, but I was wondering what your honest opinion is. If I’m travelling alone for a week, do you think a city like Rabat or Fez might be a better option for street photography, with people being a bit less confrontational? I’d really appreciate your thoughts. Thanks again for the great content.

author: @georgimihov2690
text: Old graves by/on the beach is actually a very common thing around the world - sunbathing and spending time on the beach is only a thing of the modern era. In previous centuries people had no use for beaches, as they are not arable land, so instead of creating cemeteries on good fertile land inland they would put them along a beach, or on the beach in some instances, to make use of otherwise useless space.

author: @RightToLive3222
text:
Morroco is a corrupt country. The law and constitution is broken. 

Law and order only exists if bribes are paid. 

The court system is rigged where you always fall short of their demands. 

I was a landlord in Marrakesh. I tried to evict the tenant, the case is still active even after 4 years.

This was not a murder case. This was a case of evicting a tenant from my property. 

I would urge anyone not to invest in Morroco as the people and system is corrupt and broken.

author: @DLPdiaries
text: Marrakech is so amazing! We stayed in a Riad in the Medina and did excursions to the Atlas mountains (blew our mind, so beautiful!), Essouira and a quad tour through the palmeraie. And of course wandered through the medina, had the best food and experiences in the gardens and djamaa El Fna etc. I would realy reccomend visiting. The Moroccans are SO welcoming and kind! You do need to keep in mind to haggle in the shops, and not fall for the monkey scams on the square or ' i'll show you the way' scams but other than that...such amazing people and such a beautiful culture!

author: @ArjunLSen
text: Marrakesh is boring, the people working in the tourism industry are patronising and unfriendly, and you can get really ill eating the food. It was a massive disappointment and the most overrated destination I've been to in recent years. I live in Spain and there's more historic buildings in your average Spanish town than in the city of Marrakesh. The whole place is a big sell and a big con.  Avoid Marrakesh and probably Morocco. Africa is a big place and there are a lot more interesting and better value places to visit.

author: @truthsetyoufree104
text: The destinations you've explored don't capture the full essence of Morocco. A significant portion of the population grapples quietly with challenges like unemployment, inadequate education and healthcare, poverty, and mental health struggles. Additionally, a number of individuals, driven by desperation, risk their lives attempting to migrate to Europe. This is the stark reality that some influencers may overlook or choose not to share, but global awareness is growing, and the broader truths are increasingly hard to conceal.

text: When you arrive at Marrakech Airport, Moroccan immigration officers will ask for your passport and may inquire about the purpose of your visit and the duration of your stay. Highly recommend Medina Sky for dinner a very special place with amazing service and lastly go to the Atlas Mountains specifically  go to women's Collective Dar Zite - the best Argon Oil and mountain rose perfume.  Only hint while walking in Medina stay by the walls when the scooters go by.  They will run you over. Local and tourist alike. 
TIP:
To say "enjoy" in Moroccan Arabic, you would use the phrase "b-ssaha w-rraha"

text: i just got back from a vacation in Morocco (Marrakech and Essaouira), I cant stop thinking about it, I think Morocco might be my favorite place ive  traveled to so far. My biggest advice to people is to learn to simply ignore anyone who approaches you and tries to sell you something, offer you something, or assist you with something that you didnt ask them for. You simply have to. (and I do mean literally ignore them, dont pay them attention) Learn to do this and your time will be amazing! I really really loved this place. The people are so warm and inviting, the Medinas are so interesting and endlessly entertaining, the food, oh the food! Not everywhere is the best food (think tourist traps) but if you read up on some reviews you can really find some of the most amazing foods! I'm sitting here at home missing it all! Awesome video, helped me relive my awesome trip. Recently discovered you channel and love it.Using it to prep for new adventures! THanks!

author: @FindingGinaMarie
text: Yes, paying close attention to landmarks helps a lot, and we tried to use the same paths as often as we could. It's challenging when you want to explore, because you can go deep and find yourself in a maze where everything starts to look the same! Some people suggest having a walking tour to help you navigate, but we seemed to figure it out okay on our own. By the time we did a walking tour, we had become pretty comfortable navigating it on our own. —Judy

author: @FindingGinaMarie
text:
Hi Donna, Knees and shoulders is more of a Western modesty ideal when you're heading into a church. Some Muslim women are covered head to toe. But at the same time, Marrakech and Fes and Chefchaouen are pretty tolerant of Westerners. We noticed a wide range of dress among locals and visitors alike. You don't need to wear headscarves anywhere, especially since you're not allowed in a mosque if you're not a Muslim (in Morocco). I felt my clothes were reasonably modest while also being comfortable for the heat. As long as you're not baring your midriff, showing off too much cleavage, or showing off your thighs, I think you're good. Bear in mind that some Moroccans wear more clothes because they're also trying not to tan too much/protect their skin versus exclusively for modesty purposes. We hope you have a terrific time in Morocco! —Judy
🎥 Morocco Travel Series: https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLNT98lWfwdF0_pL4S2LnqlBz2KBX9XMCc

author: @FindingGinaMarie
text: Hi Muhammed, We LOVE London. In fact our daughter was just there two days ago after visiting with us in Malta! May will be a nice time to visit Marrakech. The weather is hot but not unbearable, especially if you avoid the hottest parts of the day. And May is a month of celebrations. The Kelaa M'Gouna or Festival of the Rose runs from May 5-9 if you will be around to attend that. Have a terrific time! —Judy

author: @FindingGinaMarie
text: Hi Laura, The culture in Morocco is more modest, so if you want to respect the local culture, you won't wear clothes that are too revealing. I'm wearing tank tops, and my skirts are close to knee length, but you could cover your arms with an airy blouse and wear loose long pants or long skirts. I wasn't going to go out and buy these items since we're full time travelers, but I tried to be as modest as possible given my limited wardrobe. Morocco is reasonably understanding of tourists and don't necessarily expect them to follow all the Muslim customs. Personally, I wouldn't wear crop tops or show off my stomach or my full back or wear a bathing suit top (although we saw all of these by tourists). Does this help at all? —Judy

author: @FindingGinaMarie
text:
Hi Angela, There are no required vaccinations that the Moroccan government checks to see if you have had. However, with the monkeys in the Sahara, it wouldn't be the worst thing to have a rabies vaccination (we do not have that vaccination). We made sure that we had Hepatitis A, B, tetanus, MMR (measles, mumps, and rubella), typhoid, and cholera before we traveled to Egypt. If you have any specific concerns, it's not a bad idea to check with a travel doctor who can give you recommendations. But specific to Morocco? Nothing is required for entry. —Judy
🎥 Why we got vaccinated in Rome before heading to Egypt: https://youtu.be/ZJG4MI76u58

text: Hi Syed, You will always be at the mercy of the border agent who you talk to. Offhand, I don't recall that they asked us anything. We may have had to show proof of onward travel afterwards and possibly the address of our riad where we were staying, but nothing else. These are common questions we get asked occasionally, but I think every agent does their own thing. It can also be dependent on your passport, and we are Americans. That's not to say in these unusual times that border agents will continue to be as kind to us. —Judy

author: @FindingGinaMarie
text: We are so flattered. Thank you very much! You are right about Morocco being a perfect complement to those countries. We've been to Madrid and Barcelona, but people mentioned that Granada is a great place to visit before or after Morocco because there are certain similarities. After the Moors were expelled from Spain, many of them settled in Marrakech. Likewise certain North African dynasties once ruled parts of Spain, including Granada. We hope you have a great time! —Judy

author: @Kaedehara_egiap
text: ​@tkozikow hey there! So my best friend and I are heading to Marrakesh for my 30th birthday in July, and I was wondering if you wouldn't mind sharing a bit about your experience and places to go when we're there. We don't mind taking a day trip out of Marrakesh, so anything you can provide would be great!

author: @tkozikow
text:
​@Kaedehara_egiap we booked our excursions directly through our riad, and the highlight was a day trip to the Ourika Valley. We had a private driver who arranged for a guide in Setti Fadma to accompany us on a waterfall hike.   There are plenty of small bus/group tours, but I would highly recommend spending the extra money... It was around $175, including the tip, for two people. We stopped at a couple of Berber villages along the way and purchased some pottery and a rug for a fraction of the cost it would be in Marrakech. The guide was around $40 and well worth the money. If you decide to do this, be sure to have decent shoes that are OK for light hiking.

I suggest hiring a guide to explore the souks and key areas inside the medina. You might encounter some sales pitches along the way, but it was an interesting five hours and provided us with ideas for what to explore on our own. You can easily book either of these through a service like Viator, but if you're staying at a decent riad, they should be able to coordinate this for you.

Definitely explore the alleyways and small streets in the medina for the best food and a glimpse of the culture. Marakech is very tourist-friendly, with something like 80% of its revenue based on this, and we never felt unsafe.

author: @FindingGinaMarie
text:
Hi @rosier3188, I want to be clear that I understand your question. I did book tours when I was in Morocco (but we are full-time travelers and that's the only time that worked for us—BUT we were there 40 days and weren't in a time crunch). We recommend ONLY ever booking your excursions online for the best experience. If you wait until you show up in Morocco and go to one of the vendors shops, you might be able to get a discount, BUT you will have a much lesser experience. We talk about it in our Sahara Desert video, and although the point we make is specifically for that type of excursion, it really applies to any type of excursion. On the street, they don't care what kind of experience you give them because there's a higher likelihood that (1) you won't leave a review; (2) people who book from those vendors don't typically even look at reviews; (3) they know that they are appealing to broad masses, so the entire experience tends to be more plain than what you book in advance. That's also because the vendors know that their reputation is on the line, which isn't necessarily the case for street vendors with shops. Note that it's illegal for them to work out the details with you on the street, so they need to hustle you into their kiosk to sort out the details.

If you are referring to our Sahara Desert tour, we would suggest you book it early online and talk with the vendor in advance so that you get exactly what you want. Here is our full series, which includes tons of details about our Sahara Desert tour and the vendor we used (not affiliated in any way and make no money from recommending them). If you need me to explain it better, please let me know! —Judy
🎥 Morocco Travel Series: https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLNT98lWfwdF0_pL4S2LnqlBz2KBX9XMCc

author: @jmw-qt2ih
@FindingGinaMarie
text:
Hi again, @jmw-qt2ih! The food on the Sahara Desert tour will be the same food you get everywhere else in Morocco. Tagine will be everywhere and so will couscous. Couscous is its own meal served vegetarian with vegetables (large pieces of a few assorted vegetables like carrot, butternut squash, potato and chickpeas) or also including a choice of chicken, beef, or lamb. Tagine is basically meat with a few vegetables on top (butternut squash, carrots, and preserved lemons), which also can be vegetarian. Kevin ate a lot of kefta, which is basically beef meatballs in a tomato sauce. On the tour we also got a tomato/cucumber salad in most places. They often also had fruit as dessert, and bread was always served at meals. Our videos from Morocco show a fairly good representation of the food we ate throughout the 41 days we were in Morocco. Breakfasts would typically include yogurt, fresh fruit juice, tea, and sometimes pastries. 

Just to be a little clearer, we ate a breakfast the first day just as we were starting off at a coffee shop/grocery store for tourists and lunch was also at a local restaurant (the cost was not included in the excursion price). Dinner and breakfast the second morning was at our accommodations and included soup and was a bit fancier. Day 2 lunch was at a local restaurant (again you ordered off the menu and the cost was not included in the tour). Dinner was served family style in our desert accommodations and was very nice (chicken tagine with soup and fruit for dessert and bread). Breakfast at our desert accommodations included pastries, bread, yogurt. Lunch was at a restaurant and we again chose from a menu. 

Let me know if you have any other questions. —Judy

@priyankakamble12 Late February is considered shoulder season, which typically means that the prices are a bit lower than peak season. Marrakech and the Sahara Desert will have dry and sunny weather, although evenings and mornings will be cooler (lower 42°F (6°C)). author: @FindingGinaMarie
text:
Hi Dan, We understand that a lot of this is very cultural. I learned the hard way when I tried to take a photo of a butcher stall and the shop worker gave me the finger (and probably a few curse words). Some people in Morocco believe in the evil eye and are actually afraid of cameras. Some Muslims also have issues with "graven images" of themselves and having their picture taken is against their religious beliefs. Other people are just worried about someone copying their handiwork. Not everyone in Morocco feels this way, but we try to be respectful of those who do. 

But you are probably referring to places like the Yves Saint Laurent museum and Berber museum. We hate that, too. There were restrictions in certain places in Albania as well, and some places will allow you to take photos but not video. It's hard for us to go places that we can't share with our viewers, and we agree some of it feels petty. We've never encountered anything like Madrid where we were watched like hawks in a few of the museums there. And even when we weren't on YouTube, we still liked having photos as memories of our experiences, so thanks for relating! —Judy

author: @FindingGinaMarie
text: Hi @dotted8- For some people in Morocco there's a fear of the evil eye, for others there's a fear of having their unique ideas stolen, and others have conservative religious beliefs that make them uncomfortable with photos. We try our best to respect the local customs wherever we visit, but you are entitled not to patronize a place that doesn't allow photos. —Judy

author: @FindingGinaMarie
text: I now am realizing that you were referring to the Berber museum and the Yves Saint Laurent museum. Yeah, not taking photos was supposed to keep the art there feeling "special," and perhaps "exclusive," but it's a huge bummer for us when photos are restricted. Sorry I didn't understand this better in my original response! I was mentally more focused on all the souks and people who didn't want their pictures taken. I'd almost forgotten about the issue with the museums (out of sight; out of mind—just one more reason that they SHOULD allow photos—so you can look back on those memories later!) —Judy

text: Thank you so much for watching and for letting us know about the Majorelle/YSL Museum combo you took. We'd heard good things about the YSL Museum, but since we couldn't take photos and share them with our viewers, it had slightly less appeal for us. And we heard mixed reviews about Jardin Majorelle because it had become a bit tired looking, but we can't say if that was actually the case. I didn't have much of the kefta until we got to Fes, where I ate significantly more of it, but Kevin's were always good. Fes had a bit more spice at times, which was a happy treat, so there were differences in flavors between cities. We considered seeing more of the country than we did, but ultimately were glad we were able to be immersed in Marrakech and Fes for longer. We did a day trip to Meknes and Volubilis as a day trip from Fes as well, that we'll be sharing in two weeks. Kevin changed it up with a few burgers (including camel burgers) and also pizza a couple of times in Fes. One nice thing about Fes was that our Riad's breakfast was really delicious and satisfying, so we only needed to go out for one meal per day, which slowed some of the tagine burnout for us! —Judy

author: @ReigneOrShine
text: I said in the video that I was overwhelmed with the noise, crowd and fellow tourists. I have a love-hate relationship with the market. And you have no right to say that we are dishonest in our video. We go out as early as possible to beat the crowd,  and heat and it is always quieter in the morning. It is your fault for going out late then complain if it’s crowded. You should know the culture of the places that you visit. We also gave tips in the video, which you would know, IF you watched and listened to be careful of scammers. We knew about that by watching other travel videos and reading blogs before going. Again, it was your fault for not preparing yourself mentally of all the possibilities. Tourists are there to learn their culture, not to change it. If you did not like your experience, you have no right to accuse us of being dishonest when we clearly mentioned everything. Next time, before you talk, you listen to everything we say and have better understanding. If you do not like the crowd, go in the mountains!

author: @ReigneOrShine
text:
It's Chano here answering your comment, I am from Spain and I grew up in a Christian family, in case you may think I am Muslim because I love travelling to Morocco and learning about their history and culture. 
There is not historic evidence about a church being located where the Koutoubia Mosque was built. But our Cathedral of Sevilla was built on top of a previous mosque, and the old minaret was transformed into the beautiful Giralda bell tower, which is considered the architectural sister of the Koutoubia Minaret. 
The Spanish Christians built many churches on top of mosques after the Reconquista of Al-Andalus, and the Arabs did the same before as well, building mosques on top of Roman temples and Visigothic churches. And the Romans did the same on top of other religions temples. It was very common to repurpose materials or sites from previous religious buildings.
If you try to spread misinformation in our content we will have to block you. We respect different religions and ideas, so please do not spam our comment section with hatred. Thanks.

author: @ReigneOrShine
text:
Hi Nassa.
Thanks a million for watching our video and for appreciating our work! 🙏🏽 We're very happy to read your positive feedback and very kind words. Comments like yours keep us motivated to continue working hard and to share more travel documentaries around the world in the future. 🎥💪🏽😊

Regarding the herbal infusion, we cannot remember the price, but it wasn't expensive at all, they said it was done with mint, but not sure if something else was included. We still have some crystals at home, we use them with very little quantity and put boiling water when we have very bad hay fever for example, it helps to open the nose and it feels refreshing in the throat too. We haven't drunk it anymore though, as it was too strong, and just inhaling the boiling water it is more than enough for us. 
The name they had on their sign is: Herboriste Des Amis, and they had the following phone number on the sign too: 024382076 (Morocco's international prefix: +212). Google Maps: https://share.google/KlQ2z5FO6dCXCQQfl
We advise you contact your doctor if you have some health issues before taking traditional medicines too. Hope that helps!

We wish you all the very best to you too, have a wonderful trip to Marrakech! 🇲🇦❤️😊 Let us know if you have other questions in other videos, we are always happy to share information about travelling with our viewers. Best regards!

author: @ReigneOrShine
text: Thanks for watching. I think we have showed in the video that most local people were very friendly to us. Of course, like in all the big cities in the world, you need to have common sense. We have explained in the video about how to behave in the souks to avoid issues. Take the usual safety measures like the ones you would take visiting big cities like Rome, Paris or Madrid, just to mention some big cities we have visited in recent years, where you have to keep your belongings safe in crowded places. Do not believe scammers approaching you in the middle of the street. Do not stay alone late at night in areas where there are not many people. In our whole trip in Morocco we felt safe, you can watch the rest of our travel series to have a broader idea. We caught public buses (even at night), the train, we stayed after sunset and woke up for sunrise. Not a single issue. We met some wonderful people who just wanted to chat on the streets too. If someone suddenly offers you to bring you somewhere or to sell you something, in any country in the world, just don't trust them. If they are just curious and friendly, be open to a nice conversation. 🙂

author: @ReigneOrShine
text:
Thank you for watching our video!

We didn´t exchange money in Morocco, we have a Revolut card that allow us to withdraw money for free in other countries up to 200€ per month, and then after that we pay only 1€ commission for every withdrawal. But you have to find the proper ATMs or Banks in every country to avoid paying commissions there. In Morocco, we only used the Al Barid Bank ATMs and we never paid commission with them. I would advise you to find them in Google Maps in every city in advance to save their location, there are not that many and better be prepared with enough cash, but if you are only visiting Marrakech, you have an Al Barid Bank ATM at the airport in the departures area (walk to the right side immediately before exiting out the airport). Then you have 2 or 3 more Al Barid Bank ATMs in the Jemma el-Fnaa square just outside the Post Office building. Sometimes we could pay in Restaurants and Hotels directly with our Revolut debit card. They always have great currency exchange.

We often show dishes with meat as you can see in our video, but we remember that in almost every restaurant that we show in this video, they had vegetarian dishes as well, you can find easily falafel, vegetarian couscous or vegetarian tajine. The tacos can be found without meat as well and they often have soups and salads done with only vegetables. So I think Morocco is a good country for a vegetarian to try local cuisine as well. And don´t forget their desserts! ☺️

Hope our reply was useful. Wish you a wonderful trip to Marrakech! 🇲🇦❤️

author: @ReigneOrShine
text:
Hi Kitiwan, 
Thank you very much for watching our Morocco Travel Series and for appreciating our work. We´re really glad to know that the videos have been useful for preparing your coming trip to Morocco. You will love it there for sure! 🇲🇦❤️😊
So, ladies can wear whatever they want in public, in fact, sometimes we saw some local young women showing their legs with modern outfits, and many tourists were wearing short dresses as well. But at the same time, Morocco is a muslim country and they have certain cultural traditions and I preferred to respect that during our visit. I had heard some women receiving bad comments for dressing too sexy while visiting Morocco, but I only received smiles & nice comments about my dresses and the locals were often happy to see me with certain clothes. My husband helped me to choose some dresses before we visited, because he had already been there and he also knew the best colours for matching with the architecture 😅
In December, it may be cold early in the morning and at night too, so be careful with that, specially in the north of the country and in the desert. The Moroccan sun is very strong during the day, so traditional long clothing is actually the best for the desert for example. 
Hope my answer helped you a little bit!
All the best and wish you have a wonderful trip there!

author: @ReigneOrShine
text: Hello there Tom! There are indeed scams but for us they are very obvious. Also it was a great help that we watched a lot of videos before going there so we already knew what to avoid. For example, a random man who says that the way is closed then he will guide us to the correct way. Then he will ask money when you will arrive to the place. Do not believe them and just say no thank you. A lot of the merchants will be very friendly to you too and ask where are you from to start a conversation. We just answered them politely but we kept walking to where we were headed. If you know are lost for example in the souks because it’s like a labyrinth, just ask a merchant and probably avoid asking a random guy in the street. We were fine. We avoided “rough” areas. We used common sense. They are not going to hurt you. Some of them are just trying to earn money. Just ignore or say thank you. We felt safe in general. A lot of them are very friendly too. We met a family who just wanted to speak and practice their English. We are still in contact with them and invited us to visit their town.

author: @ReigneOrShine
text:
About the second question, Sardinia and Morocco are totally different. It depends on what you want to experience.

Sardinia is for beautiful Mediterranean coasts, coastal towns, chill life, amazing well kept nature, friendly locals, good food.

Morocco if you want to experience North African culture, more lively chaotic but unique culture, good food, mix of African and European architecture. And by the way, Moroccan architecture is impeccable and incomparable. They have one of the best designs I’ve ever seen. As a matter of fact, I want a touch of Moroccan design in my future house. Haha!! 

So if you decide which ones you want to experience, choose one. If you have more questions, feel free to ask us. We have Sardinia videos too.

author: @ReigneOrShine
text: Also, be careful of other videos describing the scams in Morocco. We found them too exaggerated. We’re not saying that scamming doesn’t exist but sometimes they were too much. And I think it is becoming more and more famous through social media so they are more careful now too.

author: @NajahAzzouzi
text: While I appreciate the attention you gave Morocco and Marrakech, I was quite annoyed by the way you handled and reported the snake charmers’ situation. Holding 20 dirhams (the equivalent of $2) in your hands, like some sort of protective strategy to get the snake charmers to let you film them alone was insulting. Then, you went on to describe the interaction like some sort of primitive encounter (that was not expected how it unfolded, you said). They took your camera to film the cobra up close, and you ended up with a great shot!! That didn’t seem to redeem them in your eyes. As to putting a snake around your neck, that’s standard practice, and, I believe, you must have researched it prior to going. I don’t think you said that out of malice, yet your “ classic Western tourist approach” is insulting and reinforces the civilizational/colonial prejudices.

The sweets you show at 9:15 are mass-produced and don't represent the exquisite Moroccan pastry.
They contain more peanuts than almonds and sugary syrups instead of honey, and the flours are usually of poor quality.
To enjoy true Moroccan pastries, you have to go to Pastiseria Le Gato in Marrakech, among many others; they're nothing like what you find in the souks.

text:
Marrakesh is like the NEw York of Morocco .  The Medina is full of tourists milling around and the Medina is a constant hum of mopeds and scooters buzzing up the middle.  She said occasional i would say constant.  

  anywhere but Marrakesh would be my suggestion.  Fez or Chefchouan for an authentic experience.   Found a hamman in Marrakesh near the Grand old Mosque.  The old islamic college worth a visit.  The Bahia Palace under construction so sights limited.  I agree with photography paradise.  stay away from snake charmers..big scam.
but remember:  ripping off tourist , or worst hurting or assaulting a tourist is jail time, no questions ask.   So no matter what they demamd from u they wont do anything to you.  Military dictatorships are great for tourists. 

Negotiating is their national sport.  You will get ripped off (relatively speaking) they are just better that you.  you will end up buying a carpet u didnt think you needed.  the best way is to sample a price for let us say a leather bag. go to 3 different shops to get a sample price then you will have an idea what the price would be .  offer half and be ready to walk away .  u gotta be kind of rude . 
as for food , go outside Medina and you will find a grill smell.  look for a big pot on counter , it means soup is available for 10 MAD.  
it is a great country ,  people are generally nice ....explore the towns outside marrakesh

author: @SueHutton
text: I see this was made in 2010. Many big changes in Tangier road systems since then and there is a project to 'join up' Tangier and Malabata bays. The view will be amazing as cruise liners come into port. If anyone is up, that is. They tend to come in before dawn. The medina is just the same. Oh, and the clubs and bars along the corniche have all been demolished for a vision of a 'clean' Tangier which is family friendly. That area will also look on to a marina. The fishing port is being shunted sideways. They're supposed to be trying to keep the walls of the Kasbah but they look very precarious.

author: @yesmean9145
text:
Let's make some things clear here:
1- Moorish architecture is THE moroccan architecture which is the local indigenous one.
2-Tangier takes its name from Tingis who was actually the daughter of Atlas king of Mauretania (ancient Morocco).
3-The blue of Chefchaouen is not recent as some houses were already painted blue decades before 1970 then it spread all over the town later on.

author: @fouedfoued5692
text:
_Tangier , indeed can boast of being one of the very few cities on earth where almost all the great civilizations have felt attracted and drawn to for they've all left their mark or legacy , from the Levant Phoenicians (more than a millennium b.c) , followed by the Greeks , then the Roman till the Syrio-Arab Umayyads...Tangier is one the few emplacements where Gods and winds collide , where Heracles defeated the God Antaeus (Greek mytho|ogy) , after that the Greek Colossus seized the Golden Apple in Lixus (Today's Larache).._
_Tangier the Silent witness of one of the biggest naval collisions between the British Nelson’s fleet (Which was frequently stationed in front of Cap Spartel) Vs the Napoleon's Franco-Spanish great Armada in the Trafalgar campaign in the Strait of G¡braltar l8o5.._
_Just a couple of stories (among others) from my beloved Tangier.._

author: @islamisthetruewaytogod6812
text:
Hello ! May Allah protect and guide you to his light and happiness in this life and the hereafter, God bless, Ameen. Excuse me for giving a little presentation of Islam, because it is very misunderstood nowadays, especially on those « Antichrist's » times, where media and politics are mixed to distort history and truth. And terrorists (puppets of the Antichrist) who misinterpret verses, out of ignorance and political motivations, and take them out of historical context (just like radical atheists do by the way) don't help either. Thank you very much for your time.

First of all, if you have any questions regarding Islam and yourself, just open the Quran randomly, and you will find the answer in front of you, like a miracle and a sign from God.
	
Islam is an arabic word that means the Surrender to the One and Only God, our Creator, Protector, Provider, who gives us life and all that we have, we are safe and sound by his will and grace, we are His and to Him we return, and we have to thank him in this trial life by submitting to him by our free will, or later in the Day of judgment when it's too late to save our own skin. Islam was the original Religion descended to earth from heaven with Adam and Eve (peace and blessing be upon them) in the beginning of humanity. and was passed to people with the succession of the 124 000 prophets and 315 messengers of God to all nations and civilizations since, passing by Noah, Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, Ismaël, Joseph, Moses, Aaron, Joshua, David, Solomon and Jesus (Peace and blessing be upon them) during the history of mankind, the last replaces and completes the previous, until the succession of the last messenger of God fourteen centuries ago, Muhammad (Peace and blessing be upon him) to complete the noble morals of all mankind, to bring humans and jinns out of darkness into light, and to purify people's religion and belief from corruption and polytheism, and return it to purity and true monotheism, like it was in the times of the prophets (Peace and blessing be upon them).

Many Religions that we know nowadays, at their beginning were true and under Islam, initiated by one of the prophets of God, but their original teachings, history and scriptures have been corrupted over time with falsification and polytheism, or lost and replaced with false ones. That's why Islam is the only Religion accepted by God nowadays, which consists in bearing witness that there is no god besides Allah (God in Aramaic, the original language of Jesus and the Gospel), and that Muhammad is His servant and messenger, just like Jesus and Moses and others are His servants and messengers. Never a messenger of God said he was God or literally son of God, it was the people after him who changed the words of God and corrupted the Religion. God is unique and absolute, He does not need to have a family and sons or to associate anyone else with His kingdom, He can simply create whatever He wants, everything belongs to Him, and to Him everything will return. Allah said in Surah Al-Mu’minun : “God has never begotten a son, nor is there any god besides Him. Otherwise, each god would have taken away what it has created, and some of them would have gained supremacy over others. Glory be to God, far beyond what they describe. The Knower of the hidden and the manifest. He is exalted, far above what they associate. (91-92 / Translated by ITANI).

Allah means the one and only God, the God of all prophets and creatures, the creator of the universe and mankind, and the Master of the Day of judgment, where our destiny, Hell or Paradise, is decided based on our faith and deeds in this trial life, and above all, Allah's mercy. 

Allah said in Surah Al-Ikhlas : In the name of God, the Gracious, the Merciful.
Say, “He is God, the One. God, the Absolute. He begets not, nor was He begotten. And there is none comparable to Him.” (1-4 / Translated by ITANI).

Allah said in Surah An-Nisa : O FOLLOWERS of the Gospel! Do not overstep the bounds [of truth] in your religious beliefs, and do not say of God anything but the truth. The Christ Jesus, son of Mary, was but God's Apostle - [the fulfilment of] His promise which He had conveyed unto Mary - and a soul created by Him. Believe, then, in God and His apostles, and do not say, "[God is] a trinity". Desist [from this assertion] for your own good. God is but One God; utterly remote is He, in His glory, from having a son: unto Him belongs all that is in the heavens and all that is on earth; and none is as worthy of trust as God. Never did the Christ feel too proud to be God's servant, nor do the angels who are near unto Him. And those who feel too proud to serve Him and glory in their arrogance [should know that on Judgment Day] He will gather them all unto Himself: (171-172 / Translated by Muhammad Asad).

Allah the Most Merciful said in Surah Ali-Imran : Behold, the only [true] religion in the sight of God is [man's] self-surrender unto Him; and those who were vouchsafed revelation aforetime took, out of mutual jealousy, to divergent views [on this point] only after knowledge [thereof] had come unto them. But as for him who denies the truth of God's messages - behold, God is swift in reckoning!
Thus, [O Prophet,] if they argue with thee, say, "I have surrendered my whole being unto God, and [so have] all who follow me!" - and ask those who have been vouchsafed revelation aforetime, as well as all unlettered people, "Have you [too] surrendered yourselves unto Him?" And if they surrender themselves unto Him, they are on the right path; but if they turn away - behold, thy duty is no more than to deliver the message: for God sees all that is in [the hearts of] His creatures.
Verily, as for those who deny the truth of God's messages, and slay the prophets against all right, and slay people who enjoin equity - announce unto them a grievous chastisement.
It is they whose works shall come to nought both in this world and in the life to come; and they shall have none to succour them.
(19-22 / Translated by Muhammad Asad)..

God said : Say, “We believe in Allah, and in what was revealed to us, and in what was revealed to Abraham, and Ishmael, and Isaac, and Jacob, and the Patriarchs, and in what was given to Moses and Jesus, and in what was given to the prophets from their Lord. We do not differentiate between any of them. And to Him, we surrender.”
(2:136 / Translated by Community)

Salam (Peace) -

author: @analuisaarkesteijntenan2538
text:
Egypt was a dream trip for my family, and I’m very happy that I’ve been to Cairo and the Valley of the Kings once in my life. Seeing the pyramids with my own eyes, standing inside them, and seeing Tutankamon, , his mask, and all those incredible treasures was truly magical. The kids absolutely loved it, and it was unforgettable to witness what that civilization achieved thousands of years ago.
However, I would never go back again. Unfortunately, tourists are constantly treated like walking bags of money. You can barely breathe without being surrounded by people asking for tips, money, or trying to sell something. Even if you already bought a papyrus or another souvenir, they keep coming and often won’t leave you alone unless you give them money. At times, it felt impossible to walk peacefully with our family and guide because there were always another 6–10 people following us everywhere. It became very exhausting and disturbing.

The guides were not much better. We paid a fortune for private guides, expecting detailed explanations inside the museums and historical sites, but instead they rushed us through everything so we would have more time (and money) to spend in shops connected to their friends or family (?). 

The beach hotels were also disappointing in this regard. Even at an all-inclusive resort, the staff constantly expected tips for every little thing — even for serving a glass of water at the swimming pool.

So yes, I would still recommend that everyone visit Egypt at least once because the history and monuments are extraordinary. But do not expect a relaxing or quiet holiday. It will be extremely hot, incredibly impressive, and at the same time, very overwhelming.

author: @raucousmortuary
text: It was disappointing to see so much misinformation about both sides of Niagara Falls. Yes, the Canada side is touristy. But it also has world-class views, many different amazing experiences (Cave of the Winds, Maid of the Mist, The Tunnel) to see the Falls up close, which is exciting as hell no matter how many others are there. Also, southern Ontario has great rural spaces including wineries, scenic gorges, and Niagara-on-the-Lake, a gorgeous region. The US side most certainly does NOT "have only one observation deck". There is one TOWER, but multiple ways to view the falls in a more natural setting than Canada including their own Cave of the Winds and Maid of the Mist. There are also many other attractions in the area" Whirlpool Park, Devil's Hole with its spectacular and challenging hikes, Fort Niagara, Power Vista, and the Aquarium. This video is full of it. Totally worth visiting especially in September when the crowds leave but everything is still open.

author: @kimmccarthy7747
text: I remember visiting the historic section of Galveston with my mom on a bus tour of Texas.  We were let out at the visitor's center and informed that they were filming a movie in the historic area.  We could go in anyway, if we stayed out of the cameras' way.  Mom and I said, let's go.  We found them filming exterior shots along one street, and located a hamburger stand behind the camera set up.  We had lunch watching them film antique cars and a tram over and over,  talked to a few extras in costume, and took pictures of the cameras and a wagon full of palm trees.  It was great.  When we got back to the visitor's center, we discovered no one else had left the building, scared off by the idea of the camera crew.  A vacation is what you make of it.

author: @sapporoichiban3612
text:
This seems like a travel guide for people with tic toc brain who don’t know how to look after their stuff and always need something to complain about. 

Yes, pickpockets are concentrated in tourist areas, maybe be prepared and you won’t be a victim. 

Yes, there are venders who try and scam with excursions, that is why you book with a travel agent, hotel or another reputable vender. 

In terms of the disappointment with places like Piza, I have been there and I found it to be fantastic because I understood what I was going to see in terms of history and there are literally dimensions of the tower available to anyone, lol. Venice was stinky and pricy but it was incredible, again in terms of history and culture. Maybe book a legit cooking class or take in some entertainment. If you go for just a bridge and a boat ride and you are mad, that is a you problem. Think outside the box, I chose to stay a few extra days in Venice.

author: @krab1791
text:
Las Ramblas, Barcelona lol in 2007 we were strolling down the sidewalk in the evening (with a 8 year old and a 15 year old) and my husband and I looked up to see a few transvestites. We immediately turned around.

But I found the subway to be more of a pick pocket hazard. I had my 8 year old by the hand as we stepped on. Immediately 2 men stepped between us. I got loud and didnt let go of my son. I also had my money in a pouch under my clothing. The two men immediately got off.

The worst pick pocket experience I had tho was in Rome near the Colosseum. We decided to take a short cut on our way back to our hotel. I was immediately mobbed by a bunch of kids one of whom had a square of cardboard which she pushed up against my chest. The idea is that while she got my attention, one of the other kids would be underneath picking my pocket. Too bad for them my money was in a pouch they didnt have access too.

I’ve been to visit the Pyramids. It was a 4 day stop as a part of our honeymoon. Luckily we had booked through travel agency so we had guides. We werent bothered by people trying to seperate us from our money. The Pyramids are a site to see.

Trevi Fountain is very crowded (thus the high rate of pick pockets). It’s very beautiful.

I’ve been to many of the places on the list. I usually enjoy myself. What it comes down to is you need to do your research and know what you are in for. None of this is really hidden. You just need to research and read reviews.

author: @johncraven9719
text:
The subject seems a non-starter.

One might think that living or realising a dream would always give pleasure to someone. But buying a product without really asking the right questions is almost bound to lead to worries, anger, disappointment.
BTW one of life's principles is "disappointment is the inevitable result of expecting too much". Many would book a cheap hôtel in order to increase their holiday spending budget or because of their youthful enthusiasm, but would be stupid to complain about the lack of comfort or quiet!
Those who travel while constantly comparing things to "how it was back home" are also bound to become narrow-minded and dissatisfied. To voyage with a sense of curiosity and discovery is a better mindset.

Then again do you want sandy beaches, mountain lakes, hiking, total relaxing, a hired car, being part of a tourist guided group, alone, with someone special? Have you got travel within the country sorted out? Do you know what currency is used (e.g. not every member country of the European Union uses the Euro as currency!)

author: @missusd1130
text: To avoid in Wisconsin: My home state: Events: The WI state fair, for much of the reasons stated here, also drinking events in Madison, for CRIME reasons, including being given knockout and rape drugs, esp. during Freak fest and the Mifflin street block party, and The Hodag Festival which turns out to be mostly a hidden Stugris drug, drunk, prostitute and hidden theft fest on hidden steroids.  If you want family friendly, county fairs are the way to go.  If you need something "more exciting", I suggest the Dells preferably out of the most active time--less expsneisve, less crowded, and way easier to schedule in the "off season" between mid August-mid June.  Yeah, avoid July visits.  Milwaukee should be avoided entirely unless you want to see things like the Betty Brin and other museums outside of crowded times.  Stay out of Milwaukee at night!  Don't park except in paid places, You car MIGHT be watched in those areas.  Most other places in WI are great including Door County, but look for a mosquito or tick report.  You'll thank me.  Biting flies can get guaranteed when those are active and some don't give a rip about DEET.  We've had a first hand experience of DEET failing to even affect ticks and biting flies, mine included a cases of Babiosis.  My recommendation, Visit in August through November to take advantage of the lovely foliage and harvest fest which can be quite family friendly and still have the brewskis you're wanting.  Easily Spring in great too, the bugs are still dead and the worst thing you'll experience is mud.  The camping is best in May and early June but in some places the ticks are out already.  August if ideal drying out and the sicks start to die out.  In SD where I live now stay away from Crazy Horse Monument.  It's not done and insanely expensive.  THey're right about Mt Rushmore, the parking is insanely expensive and don't visit on a cloudy day!  Buy a start park pass the SD state parks are all great, not just Custer and really worth visiting and has less ticks and mosquitoes than WI except during July  Outside of July and January/February and with a careful following of weather reports (because in May we get summer one day and winter the next LOL!).  If you're a cheapie, Spearfish anyone is FREE to explore!  And the city of Spearfish is a tourist paradise with not too far Rapids City having a lot of great sights, places to visit and even hiking areas that are great views and not hard to manage.

text:
Not like the brazen giant of Greek fame,
With conquering limbs astride from land to land;
Here at our sea-washed, sunset gates shall stand
A mighty woman with a torch, whose flame
Is the imprisoned lightning, and her name
Mother of Exiles. From her beacon-hand
Glows world-wide welcome; her mild eyes command
The air-bridged harbor that twin cities frame.
"Keep, ancient lands, your storied pomp!" cries she
With silent lips. "Give me your tired, your poor,
Your huddled masses yearning to breathe free,
The wretched refuse of your teeming shore.
Send these, the homeless, tempest-tost to me,
I lift my lamp beside the golden door!"


Not any more.

author: @SannaFinland
text:
When one's government warns against travelling, resonable people have lost all interest in visiting the country a long ago. Who wants to travel to a country that doesn't respect your privacy, has no respect for the rest of the world, believes in guns instead of healthcare and which citizens are flocking to other countries in panic? So much so that proper countries have tighten access of US citizens. 

Yet, so many lovely places in the world so welcomming. Why choose a destination in a hostile country?  

Even the offering in USA is very limited. Much more impressive nature in Canada that comes with sane and welcoming society. Asian touristic offering is, unlike USA, 21st century comfort. And Europe has the offering for more than one lifetime. Offering in many African and South American  countries has bypassed US offering. 

USA was never the dream destination for the majority of global travellers on curent millenium. For most Europeans it used to be the cheap flights alternative. USA has never been the most beautiful destination, rather far from it. USA used to be a popular shopping destination but in a global world same crap can be found online. Disney can be visited in France or Japan more  and combined with good food, proper sites, vibrant night life and safety. There are gambling destinations cross the world. 

Negatives on US travel, besides safety concerns, include too long distances for convenience, not that much to see or do, lack of public transportation, limited activities, low accommondation quality, horrible food quality, scams like tipping, price tags not maching the actual prices, lack of heating indoors, lack of affordable healthcare, hostile and violent culture. The cost to quality ratio is one of the worst in the world. 

At 2026 the attractiveness of USA travel in developed countries is on the same level as visiting Ethiopia, Sudan, Nigeria or Myanmar. Some do it, mainly for family or business reasons, but the majority doesn't even consider it as an option. Nor is it something that is likely to change.

text: The same here. I am going to a wedding near Vancouver Canada end of June. It was real fun, but then Trump came with his plans for restricting access for foreigners ($250 integrity fee, $100 foreigner fee for every national park, handover my usernames and passwords of every social media and email account, storing my biometrics for indefinite time etc) and on top of that his rhetoric about Canada and Greenland. And my vacation in the US flew out the window. I am now 64 and I need to wait at least 3 years before I can try again, I’ll be retired exactly that summer. author: @andersgulowsen2814
text:
I am from Norway, and have a questien to the ppl, and fans in Morocco. I have wondered about this since 1998, but never asked.
And you have a damn good team atm. Gz on that.
When Norway beat Brazil in 1998. (Yes I am 51 so I was Downtown in Oslo celbrating.)
You had the Moroccon fans that live in Norway started making trouble in Oslo. It never got out of hand too much, other than some minor scraps.
Probably because it was maybe 200 thousand ppl cheering, and 1000 pissed Moroccons. I am not joking 80-90% of Morocons was edging for a fight.
I can understand you got dissapointed, as you assumed Brazil would win or at least Draw. But that the country you actually live in, by your own choice, Goes forward, I would think that you would be happy for our sake. (For the record this is people that moved to Norway many years earlier, because they wanted to, not refugees etc).. 
This might sound long winded, but. If the tables was turned. How would you treat me, as a Norwegian, if I was living in any of the big citys in Morocco and started to spit (yes that did happen, Moroccon fans was spitting on the Norwgian flag, and sometimes people) at Moroccon flags and people. Because Norway lost due to Morocco beating another team? I suspect that would not go well for me. Tell me if I am wrong, but I doubt Moroccons would show the same restraint.

Me personally if I Moved to say Marrakesh with my family, to start a new life.


I would think. Well.. shit.. but at least Norway lost to my second favorate team. And go out in the streets to celebrate.

Are our cultures that different? 
Dont get me wrong, Moroccons are great. A summer some years back I was talking to a man From Morocco, just in the neighborhood, on a bench.
I asked, Is Tajin your national dish? Because I heard about it, and it is supposed to be insanly tasty, I can not remember he's name. But or the reply was this. Everyone it that little park between buidings was invited in 2 days (it ws 15 ppl ish) my family wil make you all Tajiin. You are all welcome.

No Norwegian would ever do that.. So maybe difference is a good thing, as long as we are willing to learn from each other.
I learned a lesson. 1 man can make a huge differnce, and change opinions that some have of another Nation to the better.

Ohh the food was AMAZING.. Happy New year and GL in the WC

author: @tomwright4450
text:
Morocco’s World Cup Victory — More Than Just a Game

Morocco winning the World Cup would be far more than just a sporting achievement — it would mark a profound shift in mindset, confidence, and national identity.

I’m 67 years old, and I still remember the legendary 1972 World Chess Championship between Bobby Fischer and Boris Spassky. Many saw it as simply a chess match, but it was so much more — it symbolized a clash between two global superpowers during the Cold War. When Fischer won, it reshaped how Americans viewed themselves: capable of mastering intellect, precision, and strategy on the world stage.

Similarly, Morocco’s rise represents more than football. For decades, France held a strong cultural and economic influence over North and West Africa — from language to commerce. But today, Morocco designs and manufactures its own cars, builds its own supply chains, and stands proudly as an economy as modern and capable as many in Europe.

By winning the World Cup — with Moroccan players and a Moroccan coach — the nation would send a powerful message: we can compete, lead, and win on our own terms.

This victory would inspire generations, boost national pride, and reinforce Morocco’s growing global presence — economically, culturally, and psychologically.

Having lived in Morocco, I can say this: it’s a remarkable country, full of warmth, creativity, and resilience. Its people, food, and culture are second to none. And now, the world is starting to see what Morocco has always known about itself.

text: Je parle en tant que franco marocaine. Le maroc est tolerant qu'avec les etrangers. Pour les autres c'est negrophobie et mysogenie ! C'est ça le maroc. Cette tolerance vis a vis des chretiens et des juifs n'existe pas pour les musulmans. Allez dans un hotel avec une femme marocaine c'est interdit, mais avec une française non. En tant que français vous avez le droit de pratiquer votre religion ou pas.  Un marocain qui ne fait pas le ramadan risque une amende. Pas vous. Les marocains aiment avoir une bonne image a l'etranger d'un pays ouvert et tolerant. Dans les faits ce n'est pas vrai. C'est un pays ultra conservateur ou l'inegalité homme femme est inscrite dans la loi (ex: heritage). C'est un peuple qui est dans sa grande majorité d'extreme droite.

author: @user-bh2ry9fw2m
text:
Le royaume du Maroc est l'un des rares pays où toutes les religions toutes les nationalités sont respectées et non stigmatisées. On y trouve des mosquées bien sur puisque c'est un pays musulman mais également des églises et des synagogues. Au Maroc musulmans chrétiens et juifs vivent en paix et en harmonie. C'est aussi un pays où on parle de plus en plus  plusieurs langues étrangères, le français l'anglais l'espagnol l'allemand... et même 
certains parlent le mandarin c'est tout de même extraordinaire 😂. Le problème en Europe ou ailleurs dire musulmans c'est englober 1,6 milliards de personnes sur terre, une cinquantaine de pays musulmans dans le monde. Et puis dire maghrébins est aussi un vrai problème, parce-que le mot "Maghreb" englobe plusieurs pays, le Maroc la Tunisie la Lybie la Mauritanie l'algerie.... tout ces peuples sont totalement différents les uns des autres. D'ailleurs il faut préciser que le Mot Maghreb (nom en arabe du Maroc) est en réalité le nom du Maroc  uniquement. Dire maghrébin c'est comme dire européen. Un italien n'a rien à voir avec un français ou un allemand.

author: @JLTINVESTMEDIA
text:
Je comprends votre point de vue, chacun vit les choses selon son expérience personnelle 🙏
Mais beaucoup de Marocains voient justement l’hospitalité, le respect et l’ouverture comme une force et une valeur profondément ancrée dans notre culture 🇲🇦❤
Être accueillant ne veut pas dire manquer de respect envers soi-même. On peut aimer et défendre son pays tout en restant bienveillant avec les autres peuples 🤝🌍
Au final, le plus important reste le respect mutuel, entre Marocains comme avec les étrangers 😊

text: Mr Pinseau, paix a son âme,  est à l'origine aussi de la ceinture verte autour de Rabat. Pour la mosquée, ce sont aussi des architectes marocains qui se répartissaient les différentes parties de la mosquée, qui in fine, a permis d'avoir un ensemble harmonieux et conforme à l'architecture marocaine sous le regard bienveillant et ''veillant'' de Mr Pinseau, qui était aussi un amoureux de l'art. Mr Pinseau tel un chef d'orchestre avec des milliers de musiciens artisans a composé une symphonie majestueuse, une œuvre magnifique et grandiose de l'art allié à la beauté.

author: @JLTINVESTMEDIA
text:
Merci infiniment pour votre message 🙏
Il nous touche beaucoup, car nous arrivons justement avec cette volonté de comprendre le pays avec humilité, sans idéaliser, mais aussi sans juger. Chaque pays a ses qualités et ses défis, et le plus important est d’apprendre à vivre avec respect et ouverture 🇲🇦

Nous découvrons chaque jour la chaleur humaine, la générosité et les valeurs familiales qui font la richesse du Maroc.
Si notre manière de voir les choses reflète déjà un esprit d’intégration, alors c’est très encourageant pour la suite 😊

Merci encore pour votre bienveillance et vos encouragements ✨

author: @JLTINVESTMEDIA
text:

author: @JLTINVESTMEDIA
text:
Je comprends votre ressenti 🙏
Le plus important est de ne jamais perdre les valeurs avec lesquelles vous avez grandi : le respect, la dignité, l’ouverture et le vivre-ensemble 🇲🇦❤

author: @JLTINVESTMEDIA
text:
Le Maroc a toujours été reconnu pour sa coexistence entre les cultures et les religions 🇲🇦✨
Mosquées, synagogues, églises, cimetières… ce patrimoine fait partie de l’histoire du pays et beaucoup de Marocains en sont fiers. Le respect, la tolérance et le vivre-ensemble sont des valeurs importantes 🙏

Concernant la France, beaucoup de personnes ressentent aujourd’hui une inquiétude face à l’insécurité et aux tensions sociales. Mais il faut aussi éviter de mettre tout le monde dans le même panier. La majorité des étrangers vivent, travaillent et respectent les lois du pays qui les accueille.

Au final, peu importe le pays, le plus important reste le respect des traditions, des lois et des habitants 🤝

author: @Mmm_O_2024
text:
@StevenNdukwu The Western Sahara has deep historical ties to Morocco that date back centuries. The region has been part of Morocco since the time of the Sultanates, and its historical and cultural connections to Morocco are undeniable. The legitimacy of Morocco's sovereignty over the Sahara is rooted in its historical governance and continuous presence in the region.

The current conflict, however, is a result of geopolitical dynamics that emerged in the mid-20th century. During the decolonization period, when Spain withdrew from the region in 1975, the situation became increasingly complicated. Algeria, in an effort to challenge Morocco’s territorial integrity, began supporting the Polisario Front, which sought to create an independent Sahrawi state. This geopolitical rivalry between Morocco and Algeria led to the ongoing conflict, with Algeria fueling separatist movements and pushing an agenda that has no historical basis.

The root cause of this conflict is not about the rights of the Sahrawi people, but rather a political and emotional struggle stemming from Algeria’s longstanding rivalry with Morocco. By backing the Polisario Front, Algeria has contributed to the prolongation of the dispute, rather than helping to resolve it. It is clear that the Moroccan people have always had strong ties to the Sahara, and the region’s integration into Morocco is both historical and legitimate.

The resolution of this issue lies not in dividing the region but in recognizing the longstanding sovereignty of Morocco over its rightful territory, and in fostering dialogue to bring peace and stability to the region. The Polisario movement, which was once used as a tool for political leverage, should now be seen in its true light: a product of external interference rather than a legitimate cause for independence

author: @ZozoBel-z5k
text:
The United Nations considers Western Sahara a non-self-governing territory whose final status has not yet been determined.
The UN does not recognize Moroccan sovereignty over the territory.

Instead, the UN supports a political process aimed at achieving a mutually acceptable solution that would allow for the self-determination of the people of Western Sahara, in accordance with UN Security Council resolutions (notably Resolutions 690, 1754, 2440, and subsequent ones).

The UN Mission for the Referendum in Western Sahara (MINURSO) was established in 1991 to organize a referendum on self-determination, giving the Sahrawi people the choice between independence and integration with Morocco — though that vote has not yet taken place due to political disagreements over voter eligibility and process.

As of now, Western Sahara remains on the UN list of non-self-governing territories, and its sovereignty is officially unresolved.

author: @Centrist1985
text:
British Visitor Here. 

I have been to the Philippines twice - in 2024 and 2026

The only other Asian country i've been to is China in 2013 and 2018-19

I think it is a pity the Philippine tourism sector lags behind other Asian countries because the Philippines genuinely has a lot to offer

In my experience, the biggest problem is poor organization.

Back in the UK now. I visited Cagbalete Island a few weeks ago with my girlfriend's family. The island has genuinely great sand, snorkeling opportunities, clear water and an impressive fire-dance show- there are a lot of ingredients for it being an ideal beach resort

But when we arrived we had to wait 3 hours before the lodge was ready - not very comfortable when you just want to get settled in- instead of taking responsibility, the guy that was supposed to manage our tour put his mother in charge!  

The food there was also lacklustre. My sister-in-law made a formal complaint back on the mainland but this is typical of shoddy management

On my first trip, we went to Mt Pinatubo only to be told that as a foreigner I couldn't enter because of a military base there. We were compensated by the company but it was a wasted day - they failed to do their research and inform us before hand. 

I don't say these things to be overtly negative. I've also had good experiences in the Philippines - my visits to the Villa Escudero Colonial Mansion estate, Intramuros in Manila and Hesus Na Kamay statue in Tiaong, the Corregidor Island excursion  among other sites were positive. 


But we also visited Ancestral Homes that only offered guided tours to groups - why lose potential customers by not offering couples too? 

I still have an overall positive feel about the Philippines - maybe I am biased, but I do think some common sense improvements can be made - better organization, more flexibility. 

In summary - 

Positive -  Underappreciated sites and a unique culture, the best buffet restaurants i've ever been to, room for lots of potential

Negative - More often than not, poor organization, lack of flexibility in appealing to tourists

To be fair, my experience probably wasn't too bad because I was always in the company of my Filipina partner and her family - I can imagine travelling alone, i'd probably be scammed as I was in China

I still love the Philippines and that is why i'd like to see the tourism sector grow

With silent lips. "Give me your tired, your poor,
Your huddled masses yearning to breathe free,
The wretched refuse of your teeming shore.
Send these, the homeless, tempest-tost to me,

author: @بلال-ط3خ8ن
text:
يجب على المغرب يجعل المغرب اخضر دالك بغرس الملايين الأشجار يوميا من طرف الحكومة والشعب المغربي ومساعدة الطلبة والتلاميذ في الغرس كل اخر اسبوع تم مساعدة السجناء في الغرس وتهيييء الشتلات تم يجب استعمال الطاءرات المسيرة التشجير بطاءرات المسيرة الدرون بنقل بدور الاشجار المثمرة والغير متمرة والغابيوية والأزهار وبدور الفرصة وزرعهم في المدن والقرى والصحراء وجبال الأطلس وجبال الريف والقرى والصحراء أن الفصة تقضي على الصحراء والتصحر وتجعلها خضراء  إضافة أنها تصلح العلف المواشي  يجب زرع البدور الأشجار المثمرة والغير متمرة والغابيوية وبدور الفصة في كل  المغرب  واستيراد بدور و شتلات اشجار من الدول غابات الامازون والدول الأفريقية والأسيوية وأوروبا واستيراد الارانب من استراليا وتركها في جبال الأطلس وجبال الريف والغابات وعمل حداءق خضراء في المدن المغرب كله وهدم  الدواوير ديال التراب وبني منازل جديدة  ونقل سكان الجبال الأطلس وجبال الريف  إلى المدنةالكبيرة  أو نقلهم إلى المدن وهدم المدى القديمة وجعلها ومكانها مدن جديدة  أن المغرب ادا كان جديد حديت واخضر هدا يجعل المغرب جميل وعالمي ويجلب  100 مليون  ساءح واكتر يجب التسريع في المشاريع 
الاستثمارات الأجنبية في المغرب يجب أن تختاروا المشاريع  المراد إنجازها في فترة زمنية قليلة وتمن أقل ولو بدون  استفادة من فرص العمل أو نقل تكنولوجيا لان التكنولوجيا تكتسب محليا من المخترعين والتخطيط كما فعلت الصين وكوريا الجنوبية كانوا من الدول العالم التالت
لا تعطوا الاستشارات لشركات أجنبية تعمل المشروع في مدة زمنية أطول وتمن باهظ متل استثمار شركة الفرنسية في مشروع تيجيفي التي طال انتظاره اكتر من سبع سنوات هدا كتير جدا  وبتمن باهضة لو عملته الصين او كوريا الجنوبيه أو اليابان لكان يتمن أقل سوف يعملون في سنتين فقط لهدا يجب اختيار الشركات التي تعمل السكك الحديدية والقطارات السريعة من شركات التي تكون أقل فترة زمنية في إنجاز المشروع وتمن أقل أما ادا قبلوا كدالك نقل التجربة والتكنولوجيا وفرص العمل فيكون اجمل لكن الأهم هو السرعة التنفيذ وأقل تمن في جميع المشاريع يجب أن يكون هدا الشرطين لان الدول تبقى متأخرة بسبب ضياع الوقت متلا مشروع يتم إنجازه في ستين طويلة والآخر كدالك فان إجيال من الشعوب لا تستفيد من هده المشاريع كل شيء يجب أن يكون بسرعة كبيرة سكك حديدية قطرات سدود طرق ومطارات جميع المشاريع هكدا تقدمت الصين وكوريا الجنوبية واليابان وسنغافورة
لو اخدت الصين مشروع TGV التي عملته الشركة الفرنسية بتمن باهض جدا واستغرق مدة إنجازه اكتر من تماني سنوات 
لو اخدته الصين أو كوريا الجنوبية اوسنغافورة لعملوه  في سنة واحد بأقل تمن وتعمل  ليك تلات أو اربع خطوط سكك حديدية بتمن اللدي اخدته الشركة التي عملت tgv
أن المستعمر لا يريد لمستعراته أن يتقدم لهدا يطول مدة لا لإنجاز  بتمن باهض  وخطف المشروع من دول أخرى متل الصين  ممكن تعمل ليك مشارك أخرى تلات سكك  بنفس التمن في سنة واحدة 
من يريد تسريع تطور البلد يعطي مشاريع الصين اليابان وكوريا الجنوبية وسنغافورة
هدا احسن شيء هو انتاج الطاءرات المسيرة روبوتات مدنية وعسكرية وزراعية   في مصانع  وربوتات مسيرة متل ما فعلت اكرانيا أصبحت قوية صناعيا بسبب جعل مصانع السيارات تصنع المسيرات والروبوتات والسيارات في أن واحد
